channel.title: AI on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in AI on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: The Future Of Generative AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the future of generative AI And already I know people’s hackles are rising.

But I’m going to make this a fun Friday because I want to talk about what I think is coming down the pipe. And I wanted to talk about this because, despite the fact that I know many of you hate it’s coming. Tech technology has never been bottled up once it’s been created. So it’s coming. It is going to arrive And I’m going to base what I think is going to show up in both the game industry and beyond on watching technology change in the game industry for the last 45 years and my own PhD work in artificial intelligence in the late 80s and early 90s. So I’ve already talked about artificial intelligence in general and my experience working on it in a video called artificial intelligence, which is linked in the description. I’ve also talked about the effects of artificial intelligence and technology in general on the game industry and other industries where it has actually disrupted them and eliminated them. There are industries that existed when I was a child that don’t exist anymore.

item.title: Having Fun With AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about having fun with AI.

I’ve talked before about how I view AI as being a tool and, yes, there’s concerns about how it’s going to affect the market. But I have never been able to do art, so I started playing around with meta AI. It’s the AI That’s part of your Facebook Messenger. There’s a tab in it you can click. I’d never use it. A friend told me about it and apparently it lets you can upload a few photos of yourself and let, and it can scan your face in 3D and then it will let you generate images just of yourself, and I thought, well, this could be a lot of fun. You know, as someone who’s never been able to get a single piece of artwork into any of his games, not even a mouse cursor — I wanted to try this and, as you will see, I’m going to show you images I created. It started mundane and simple, and then, because of the way my brain works, it got weird. So let’s take a look at it. This is a fun Friday. This is just for fun.

item.title: Enemy AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about enemy Ai, and this is to answer a question from driver 3899, who asked: any chance you could talk a little bit about how you approach thinking about enemy AI?

Is there a sweet spot between too complex and too simple in terms of behavior? Can you accidentally make enemies too smart for players, or is the performance hit the limiting factor and how complex you can make their behaviors? What are the most important things to keep in mind when designing the enemy AI?

item.title: Technology Changes Jobs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how technology changes jobs.

Now this is going to be kind of a companion video to a couple videos I did before and they’re linked below. It’s called Uh — industry changes and artificial intelligence because it’s sort of an extension to those, and I’m going to refer to some stories I tell in those uh, so you may want to watch them, so you understand the references I give in this video because I don’t want to be too repetitive. I know I’ve been told I’m repetitive repeatedly but I’m trying to avoid that.

item.title: Artificial Intelligence
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about AI.

There’s been a lot of people ask me my opinion on the rise of artificial intelligence in tools that are being used more and more frequently in game development and lots of other things. These are things like the llms, like chatbot or chat GPT, which are text based, or the art generators like dolly or stable diffusion. First off, let me say, while I do have a graduate degree in AI, it is from the 90s, so I am 30 years behind. Actually, wow, no, it’s an 89.. I am 34 years behind. This stuff, but we had neural Nets back then. I think a big thing about these new llms is just their sheer size and the training sets are better. Yes, a lot of stuff is advanced, but, given their nature, I kind of view them in the same light. I viewed neural Nets back then, which are — they are great tools. One issue with neural Nets is you.

item.title: Arcanum AI
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to answer some questions that have come up about how the AI worked in Arcanum.

Before I start, though, I should think it’s funny. I just got a Google or YouTube analytics email, because I’ve been doing this for more than 90 days, where it was telling me what videos were popular and what weren’t, to try to give me advice of what to do and the advice seems to be skewed towards doing things that get more people to watch ads, because it told me these technical ones — well, those attracted less viewers and, according to YouTube, they don’t think people want that. Which ones do they want? Why I quit Fallout 2? Why I quit WildStar?

item.title: Troika's Secret Fourth Project
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me Tim.

Hey, I want to talk to you about troika’s fourth project, secret project. I’m not talking demo here, I’m talking paid project that we completed. Before I can do that, let me give you a little context. So I moved to California in the late 80s from Virginia to go to graduate school at University of California, Irvine. I got my masters in 89 in computer science with a specialty in artificial intelligence, with a subspecialty in integrated learning methods. But I started on my PhD a couple years later. I went — no, not going to do this — and I went to go work at interplay. That was in 91., so in 2004, when we were finishing up vampire Bloodlines — I think it was September of 2004 — I got approached by a friend of mine who had graduated from UCI with a PhD and was working for the US Department of Defense. He had developed the system and he wanted to know if I could take tempo of Elemental Evil and write an API programming interface so that an external AI could run the game. And I was like I think I could do that and he goes, we’ll pay you.
